exemplar.title: To what extent can Boeing improve corporate governance to rebuild stakeholder trust after the 737 MAX crisis?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent can Boeing improve corporate governance to rebuild stakeholder trust after the 737 MAX crisis?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Consistent and explicit framing of ethics throughout major sections
Strong contextual integration linking Boeingâs governance failures to the ethical concept
Actionable recommendations (independent oversight) grounded in ethical accountability
weaknesses: A few analytical passages focus on financial metrics without explicit ethical linkage
SWOT elements occasionally miss tying back to ethics in every subsection
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although some financial discussions are implicitly ethical, the key concept of ethics is comprehensively woven through introduction, analysis, tools and conclusion. The analysis consistently returns to how Boeingâs decisions impacted ethical oversight and stakeholder trust, meeting the descriptor for full integration (band 5).

strengths: Visuals directly support critical arguments (ratio table underpins liquidity discussion; stakeholder matrix informs governance analysis)
Captions and inâtext explanations provide depth for most figures
weaknesses: Exceeded recommended number of documents, reducing coherence
Some diagrams (MCAS schematic, SWOT) are descriptive without deeper analytical annotations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite having more than five visuals (penalizing the numberâof-documents strand), the documents are highly relevant and sufficiently in-depth. The student explains each figureâs role in the argument, justifying a middle-band score (band 3) for relevance and depth overall.

strengths: Appropriate selection of SWOT, stakeholder matrix and ratio analysis covering qualitative and quantitative dimensions
Clear explanations of tool relevance, especially linking stakeholder power/interest to governance and ethics
weaknesses: Lacks advanced or governance-specific models that could deepen analysis
Financial ratios are described without benchmarking against peers
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses a solid set of standard business tools and applies them clearly to the research question. However, application depth varies and additional frameworks (e.g. corporate-culture models) would be needed for the top band. This aligns with an overall band 3.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Effective use of pre- and post-crash financial data to illustrate governance failures
Qualitative quotations from stakeholders vividly linked to ethical implications
Analysis moves beyond description to explain causeâeffect relationships
weaknesses: No benchmarking against industry averages limits robustness
Alternative strategies and feasibility evaluations are only briefly addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The data selection is sufficient and the narrative delivers mostly effective analysis, integrating ethical considerations with financial and stakeholder evidence. While it stops short of the sustained, multi-dimensional critique needed for band 5, it clearly meets band 4 descriptors.

strengths: Conclusions directly reflect the evidence presented throughout the IA
Explicitly answers the research question with clear governance recommendations
weaknesses: Recommendations lack specific implementation timelines and measurable cultural metrics
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusions are fully consistent with the evidence and explicitly address the research question, satisfying the top band for this criterion (band 3).

strengths: Logical flow with clear headings and a detailed table of contents
Each section follows a coherent sequence from introduction through conclusion
weaknesses: Some paragraphs (e.g., regulators vs. investors) could be better delineated for clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report is appropriately structured throughout, using standard IA conventions and supporting navigation, fulfilling the descriptor for band 2.

strengths: All required IA elements present (title page, word count, research question, visuals, appendices)
weaknesses: Repetitive sentence openings in places reduce readability
Some captions could be more analytical in focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission includes every required element with clear formatting and presentation, meeting the criteria for band 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The IA follows a logical structure: introduction, methodology, tool-based sections, conclusion, references and appendices. Headings and a table of contents support navigation, reflecting an âappropriate structure throughout.â

Each document directly supports discussion (e.g., ratio table underpins liquidity analysis; stakeholder matrix feeds governance argument). Explanations accompany figures, showing sufficient depth, but some visuals (e.g., MCAS diagram) are descriptive with limited analytical extraction, and there is repetition between text and table. This aligns with âdocuments are relevant and sufficiently in-depthâ rather than the wider range and deeper synthesis expected for band 4.

All required IA elements are present: title page with word count, research question, key concept, sequential page numbers, references, appendices and visuals. Thus the work meets the criteria for band 2.

The IA contains at least six distinct supporting documents/visuals (SWOT image, stakeholder matrix, MCAS diagram, financial-ratio table, cancelled-orders table and market-segment graphic). As rubric wording assigns band 1 when âonly 1-2 documents OR more than 5 documentsâ are provided, the presence of more than five places the work in band 1 despite adequate relevance.

Tools are applied to the research question: SWOT highlights ethical weaknesses, stakeholder matrix links power/interest to governance reforms, and ratios quantify crisis impact. Yet application depth variesâstakeholder discussion drifts into description, and financial analysis stops at ratio movement without benchmarking. Relevance is clear but effectiveness is uneven, matching the descriptor where ârelevance not always clear but adequately applied.â

Data include pre- and post-crash financial ratios, order-cancellation statistics and qualitative stakeholder quotations. These datasets are appropriate and more than superficial, but do not span multiple years nor compare with competitors, limiting robustness. This aligns with âadequate selection and useâ (band 3).

The narrative moves beyond description, explaining how ratio deterioration evidences governance failure and how stakeholder power necessitates reforms. Ethical implications are linked back to tools, showing mostly effective integration. Nonetheless, evaluation of alternative strategies or their feasibility is brief, so it falls short of the sustained, thorough integration expected for band 5.

The research question is explicitly answeredâBoeing can rebuild trust to a significant but qualified extent through specified governance changes. The answer is clear and directly tied to the preceding analysis, satisfying the top-band descriptor for this short criterion.

The student selects standard but appropriate HL tools: SWOT, power-interest matrix and ratio analysis. This meets an âadequate selectionâ because they cover both qualitative and quantitative angles; however, strategic or governance-specific frameworks (e.g., Mendelowâs matrix variant, corporate-culture models) are absent, preventing classification as âeffectiveâ (band 4).

The conclusion reiterates financial evidence and stakeholder needs, advocating measurable reforms; recommendations align with data presented. No new unsupported claims are introduced, fulfilling âconclusions fully consistent with evidence.â

All analysis is anchored in Boeingâs specific context: detailed description of market share, cancelled orders, liquidity shifts, stakeholder power mapping and governance reforms. Diagrams, tables and narrative always return to how the issue affects Boeing and its stakeholders, showing comprehensive, organisation-wide integration. The depth and frequency of these links match the descriptor of a "comprehensive analysis fully integrated throughout" (band 5).

The student consistently frames the investigation around the key concept of ethics, linking the 737 MAX crashes to governance failures, profit-over-safety culture and the need for transparent oversight. Ethical language appears in every major section (SWOT, stakeholder matrix, ratio discussion and conclusion). However, the concept is not woven into every analytical sentence, and some passages focus purely on financial performance with only an implicit ethical link. This demonstrates a strong, but not fully seamless, integrationâconsistent with the band descriptor for 4 marks rather than the fully integrated standard required for 5.

annotations.comment: The research question is clear but could be refined by defining specific metrics for âstakeholder trustâ to ensure measurable analysis.
Identification of âEthicsâ as the key concept is explicit and provides a strong thematic anchor for the entire assessment.
The weaknesses discussion cogently links governance flaws to ethics, offering clear analytical depth.
The conclusion concisely restates key governance failures and ethical imperatives, providing a coherent summary.
Recommendations lack specific implementation timelines; include time-bound milestones to strengthen accountability.
There are over five supporting documents; consider selecting the most relevant three to five for coherence and depth.
The detailed table of contents effectively structures the report and aids navigation through major sections.
The introduction provides strong contextual background on the 737 MAX crisis and establishes relevance to Boeingâs ethical oversight.
Acknowledging potential bias in qualitative sources is good, but the student should critique specific examples to demonstrate evaluative depth.
The methodology section ends midâsentence. Complete the description of financial ratio analysis to clarify the intended quantitative approach.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The SWOT narrative explains the tool but does not explicitly integrate the ethical concept into each SWOT element.
The SWOT figure would benefit from a legend or annotations highlighting ethical vulnerabilities rather than generic labels.
The caption âFigure 1: SWOT analysisâ is descriptive but could highlight key ethical insights uncovered in the analysis.
The SWOT strengths section cites market share but misses linking this to ethical governance practices.
The external threats section discusses competition but should explicitly tie these pressures to ethical scrutiny and compliance demands.
The recommendation for independent oversight committees is both actionable and highlights ethical accountability well.
The proposal for ethics training is sensible but would be stronger with quantitative evidence or case study comparisons.
The call for cultural reform is clear but would benefit from specifying measurable cultural metrics and indicators.
The stakeholder matrix introduction effectively justifies the toolâs relevance to governance and ethics.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Figure 2 caption is generic; enhance it by summarizing key ethical concerns and stakeholder priorities.
The discussion on regulators and investors runs together; consider reorganizing into separate paragraphs for clarity.
Quotations from employees vividly illustrate stakeholder perspectives and strengthen the ethical analysis.
The section would be more robust if quantitative metrics (e.g., survey scores) were added to track stakeholder engagement improvements.
Several sections begin with similar phrasing, which reduces readability; vary sentence openings to enhance flow.
The financial formula definitions are correctly formatted and clearly explained, demonstrating precise tool application.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The ratio table provides data but would be more informative with industryâaverage benchmarks for comparison.
The description of the 2018 ratios is solid but could tie the financial trends more explicitly to their ethical and trust implications.
The analysis of 2019 ratio declines effectively uses data to show corporate governance failure and financial distress.
Mentioning scenario planning and stress testing is valuable; further evaluate feasibility, costs, and expected impact on trust restoration.
